summary: DHH reflects on the release of The Rails Documentary by Honeypot, marking twenty years of Ruby on Rails. He acknowledges the collaborative effort of thousands of contributors and the framework's impact, from tiny apps to Shopify's $77 billion business. Turning 44 prompts him to consider legacy and how to measure a life well-spent, drawing on Stoicism and Seneca's thoughts on mortality. He concludes that Rails has been unequivocally worth the effort, pushback, and drama.
key_points: The Rails Documentary prompted a rare moment of looking back on twenty years of Ruby on Rails development
Rails has scaled from small internal apps to powering $77 billion companies like Shopify, with over a million applications built
Turning 44 and passing life's statistical halfway point brought reflections on legacy and mortality
Stoicism and Seneca's philosophy on the shortness of life frame how DHH measures whether his time was well-spent
money_quotes: Together with 37signals, Rails has been my life's work.
I've been humbled to see well over a million applications be built with it. Everything from tiny internal apps to $77 billion behemoths like Shopify.
One of the reasons I don't spend too much time looking back is because how easily you might end up stewing in your own nostalgia and self-satisfaction.
That's past the halfway point for the average human in every society around the world.
To believe that I spent my time well, in service of pursuits worth the effort, in concert with people worth the relation, in honor of virtues worth upholding.
Looking back at Ruby on Rails, I can easily and emphatically say 'totally worth it'. Worth the effort, worth the pushback, worth the dramas, worth the impact.
key_insight: Twenty years on, Rails stands as a life's work DHH can look back on with the conviction that the effort, conflict, and collaboration were entirely worth it.
